title: Ramesh Kumar Sharma v. Hindustan Polymers Ltd. & Anr.
court: High Court of Judicature at Allahabad
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was effected without conducting a proper domestic inquiry as mandated under the Standing Orders and principles of natural justice.
The charge sheet issued to the petitioner was vague and did not specify the exact nature of misconduct, thereby vitiating the entire disciplinary proceedings.
The petitioner had an unblemished service record of 18 years and the punishment of dismissal was disproportionate to the alleged misconduct.

The respondent employer failed to provide the petitioner an opportunity to cross-examine the management witnesses during the inquiry, violating Article 311 principles applicable through service jurisprudence.
The termination order was passed with mala fide intent as it coincided with the petitioner's active participation in legitimate trade union activities.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Allahabad allowed the writ petition filed by the petitioner-employee, holding that his dismissal from service by Hindustan Polymers Ltd. was illegal and void ab initio. The Court found that the domestic inquiry was conducted in gross violation of principles of natural justice and that the punishment of dismissal was shockingly disproportionate. The respondent company was directed to reinstate the petitioner with full back wages and continuity of service.
judgmentText:
JUDGMENT

Hon'ble Justice Priya Shankar Mishra

Heard learned counsel for the parties at length. This writ petition under Article 226 of the Constitution of India has been filed challenging the order of dismissal dated 14.03.2021 passed by the respondent No.1, Hindustan Polymers Ltd., against the petitioner Ramesh Kumar Sharma, who was employed as a Senior Technician Grade-II.

Brief facts, as emerging from the record, are that the petitioner had been in continuous service of the respondent company for approximately 18 years with no adverse entries in his service record prior to the impugned proceedings. On 02.01.2021, a charge sheet was issued to the petitioner alleging insubordination and causing loss to company property. The petitioner submitted his reply denying all charges. A domestic inquiry was conducted by an Inquiry Officer appointed by the management, and on the basis of the inquiry report, the petitioner was dismissed from service vide order dated 14.03.2021.

Learned counsel for the petitioner has vehemently argued that the entire inquiry proceedings were vitiated on account of multiple procedural irregularities. It has been contended that the charge sheet was couched in vague and general language without specifying the date, time, and manner of the alleged misconduct, thus denying the petitioner a meaningful opportunity to prepare his defence. Further, it has been submitted that the management witnesses were examined behind the back of the petitioner and he was not afforded the right to cross-examine them, which is a fundamental requirement of a fair inquiry.

Learned counsel for the respondent company has sought to justify the termination by relying upon the inquiry report and has argued that due procedure was followed. However, this Court finds merit in the contentions raised on behalf of the petitioner.

Upon perusal of the inquiry record placed before this Court, it is evident that the Inquiry Officer proceeded to record the statements of management witnesses in the absence of the petitioner. The petitioner's representative made a specific request to be permitted to cross-examine the witnesses, which request was summarily rejected without any cogent reason. This Court is of the considered opinion that the right to cross-examine witnesses in a domestic inquiry is not a mere formality but goes to the root of the matter and its denial renders the entire inquiry null and void. Reliance is placed upon the settled proposition of law laid down by the Hon'ble Supreme Court in a catena of decisions including Workmen v. Bharat Fritz Werner Ltd.

Furthermore, this Court notes with concern that the charge sheet dated 02.01.2021 does not disclose the specific incidents constituting the alleged misconduct. The charges are omnibus in nature and do not put the petitioner on notice of what exactly he was required to answer. This is a fundamental defect which goes to jurisdiction.

On the question of proportionality of punishment, this Court finds that a person with 18 years of unblemished service cannot be visited with the extreme penalty of dismissal on charges that are not only vague but have been proven through a vitiated inquiry. The punishment is shockingly disproportionate and cannot be sustained.

In view of the foregoing, the writ petition is allowed. The order of dismissal dated 14.03.2021 is hereby quashed and set aside. The respondent No.1 is directed to reinstate the petitioner in service with full back wages and continuity of service within a period of six weeks from the date of this order. The petitioner shall also be entitled to all consequential service benefits including seniority and provident fund contributions for the intervening period.

No order as to costs.

(Justice Priya Shankar Mishra)
Allahabad
Dated: 17.07.2023
